q: A support worker pockets a receipt after a 10pm shift. What most likely happens?
options: They submit it via app that night
It goes in the wash and is never seen again
They email it to finance the next morning
They log it in the house notebook
explanation: Uniform goes in the wash, receipt disappears. Finance will chase for weeks. This is the rule, not the exception.

q: Why is wrong GL coding a compliance problem in NDIS — not just an admin headache?
options: It slows down accounting software
Participant funding gets charged incorrectly, breaching NDIS obligations
It makes the CFO report look bad
It delays payroll
explanation: Coding errors in NDIS orgs can mean a participant's budget gets charged for things it shouldn't. That's a regulatory breach, not a spreadsheet problem.

q: One card, four staff, mystery $340. What's the worst consequence?
options: Finance has to make an extra call
The amount is too small to matter
They cannot produce an audit trail — fraud risk and audit exposure
The wrong cost centre gets charged
explanation: Shared cards eliminate accountability entirely. If an auditor asks who spent $340 and why, finance literally cannot answer.

q: A staff member makes a personal purchase on the company card. The manager declines it in the approval system. What happens to the money?
options: It gets reversed to the company account
Nothing — the money left when the card was tapped
Finance recovers it from next payroll
The bank reverses it automatically
explanation: All card approvals are post-spend. The money left the moment the card was tapped. Declining creates a record for disciplinary action — but doesn't reverse the spend.

q: The GM goes on leave for two weeks. What happens to the approval queue?
options: Another manager covers it
It stops — 200 transactions pile up, rubber-stamped or reviewed too late
Transactions auto-approve after 48 hours
Finance approves instead
explanation: One person = one point of failure. When they're gone the queue dies. When they return: rubber stamp or days of review. Neither is a real control.

q: What date does finance typically get a clean view of last month's spend?
options: Last day of the month
1st-3rd of next month
14th-15th of next month
Within a week
explanation: Halfway through the next month. Halfway through November before October is closed. By then they're already deep in November's chaos.

q: Month-end is a ___________ on every broken process above it.
explanation: Month-end isn't a separate problem. It's the accumulated cost of missing receipts, wrong coding, shared cards, and broken approvals all hitting at once.

q: Shared cards destroy accountability and ___________ trails.
explanation: Without individual attribution there is no audit trail. You can't reconstruct who spent what when the information was never captured.

q: On a corporate card, a declined approval is a ___________ tool, not a financial control.
explanation: Declining creates a paper trail — proof the spend wasn't authorised. That supports disciplinary action. But the money is already gone. Real card controls are pre-spend: limits, restrictions, individual attribution.

context: Finance manager: We tried to tighten up our expense process last year. Put in a new policy, sent it to all staff. It lasted about two months then everything went back to how it was.
options: That's unfortunate — what policy did you try?
That's exactly what we hear from every org. The problem isn't the policy or the staff — it's that you're relying on non-finance people to do finance tasks correctly under pressure. That never sticks.
You probably need a stricter policy and better enforcement.
It sounds like a training issue — have you tried more frequent reminders?
explanation: Acknowledge the norm, then reframe. The problem is system design. Behaviour fixes don't stick because the system itself is broken.

context: Prospect: We've got about 40 support workers across 8 houses. They use a shared card at each house. It works okay for the most part.
options: How many transactions per month?
If I pulled up any one of those transactions right now, could you tell me which of the 40 staff made that purchase?
Have you considered virtual cards?
What bank are you with?
explanation: If the answer is no — and it almost always is — the entire accountability problem is exposed in one answer. Don't attack 'it works okay', just probe underneath it.

context: Finance manager: We have a solid approval process — every expense gets reviewed by a manager before we process it.
options: Great — how long does that take?
Is that mostly card transactions or expense reimbursements? Because they work pretty differently — on card spend the money is already gone before it hits the approval queue.
How many approvers do you have?
What system do you use for that?
explanation: Card approvals are post-spend — the money is already gone. Reimbursement approvals have real teeth. Most people don't know the difference. This question opens that up.
